data.title: Fidelity Balanced Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

U.S. stocks gained 8.12% in the third quarter, according to the S&P 500® index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April. The advance has been supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reduction since December. Amid a favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed September just below its all-time high. Growth stocks continued to lead in Q3, while smaller-cap shares outpaced large-caps, signaling a broadening of market leadership and increased investor appetite for risk.

Stocks entered July on an uptrend, gaining 24.92% from April 9 through midyear. The rally was sparked by a 90-day pause on most tariffs announced a week earlier by the Trump administration. Momentum carried into Q3, supported by strong corporate earnings and a resilient U.S. economy – largely driven by massive AI-related outlays by big tech companies. Renewed expectations for Fed rate cuts also supported stocks. On September 17, the central bank lowered its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points, shifting from its wait-and-see stance. The decision reflected concerns about labor-market softness, which outweighed a rise in inflation – up 2.9% year-over-year in August.

The S&P 500® rose 2.24% in July, buoyed by the passage of a massive tax and spending package in Congress and President Trump's extension of the deadline for reciprocal tariffs. The index gained another 2.03% in August following news that the U.S. economy grew 3.3% in the second quarter, driven by trade-related momentum, increased business investment and strong consumer spending. On September 25, Q2 gross domestic product was revised upward to 3.8%, marking the fastest pace since the third quarter of 2023. The S&P 500® advanced 3.65% in September, lifting its year-to-date gain to 14.83%. From April 9 through September 30, the index gained 35.07%.

In Q3, the index was driven by the growth-oriented information technology (+13%) and communication services (+12%) sectors – reflecting the transformative potential of generative artificial intelligence – as well as in consumer discretionary (+10%). The much smaller utilities sector gained roughly 8%, boosted by AI-driven demand for electricity to power data centers. In contrast, defensive-oriented stocks trailed the broader market: consumer staples returned -2.36%, while health care and real estate gained 4% and 3%, respectively. Other notable laggards included financials (+3%), materials (+3%) and industrials (+5%). Lastly, energy rose 6%, despite a decline in the price of oil stocks.

Turning to fixed income, U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 2.03% in Q3, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Longer-dated bonds with a maturity of 10+ years posted the strongest advance, at 3.26%, while intermediate and short-term issues lagged, with 7-10 year bonds gaining 2.58%, 5-7 year maturities up 1.82%, and 1-3 year issues rising 1.20%. By credit rating, bonds rated BAA (the Bloomberg equivalent to BBB) outperformed higher-quality issues.

Results at the sector level reflected a positive tone for risk assets, with yield-advantaged, credit-sensitive sectors broadly outperforming Treasuries (+1.51%). U.S. corporate bonds (+2.60%), buoyed by a strong earnings season, led among investment-grade categories, followed by agency mortgage-backed securities (+2.43%) and government-related bonds (+2.23%). Commercial mortgage-backed securities (+1.75%) and asset-backed securities (+1.64%) more narrowly outpaced Treasuries.

Outside the Aggregate index, U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities gained 2.10%, per Bloomberg, while U.S. high-yield corporate bonds (+2.54%) finished slightly below investment-grade corporates. Notably, emerging-markets debt delivered the bond market's best returns for the quarter – rising 3.58% for investment-grade and 3.09% for high-yield issues. ■

Performance Review

For the three months ending September 30, 2025, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 6.25%, topping the 5.66% advance of the Fidelity Balanced Hybrid Composite Index SM .

Within the portfolio, equities represented about 65% of assets, on average, versus the 60% average stock allocation in the Composite index in Q3. Meanwhile, investment-grade bonds comprised roughly 35% of the fund, on average, compared with the Composite index weighting of 40% this past quarter. The remainder of the portfolio was comprised of a cash position of less than 1%. The fund did not hold any high-yield bonds this period. The risk-on backdrop for capital markets that began in early April, after the Trump administration paused tariffs for most countries, carried over into the third quarter. As a result, stocks significantly outperformed investment-grade bonds. This meant that the fund's outsized exposure to equities and underweight in investment-grade debt aided performance relative to the Composite index. With that said, security selection among the fund's equity investments was by far, the biggest relative contributor these past three months.

More specifically, the portfolio's stock holdings rose 8.80% for the period, versus 8.12% for the S&P 500 ® index. Picks in the communication services and information technology sectors helped most. On an individual basis, an overweight stake in Alphabet (+38%) led the way the past three months, as the advertising giant and parent of the Google search engine continued to execute on its artificial intelligence strategy and produce solid financial results. Management highlighted that demand for AI products boosted quarterly sales, justifying an increase in capital spending. Meanwhile, regulatory headwinds eased following an antitrust ruling in September that allowed Google to keep key assets, including its Chrome browser and Android ecosystem, further boosting the stock. Alphabet was a top-5 holding at the end of September.

Out-of-index exposure to Belgium-based biopharmaceutical firm UCB (+40%) also helped. On July 31, the company posted quarterly revenue and profitability that exceeded analysts' expectations, driven by much stronger-than-expected sales for its psoriasis treatment, Bimzelx ® . The stock gained steadily through the rest of Q3, then jumped again just before quarter end after MoonLake Immunotherapeutics announced disappointing trial results for its hidradenitis suppurativa drug, which had been viewed as a potential key competitor to Bimzelx.

On the other hand, investment choices in consumer discretionary detracted this quarter. In stock-specific terms, a non-index stake in Circle Internet Group (-27%) pressured relative performance the most. The portfolio held a position in the company privately through the initial public offering on June 5, shortly after which it appreciated significantly. However, after peaking later that month, the stock pulled back in Q3. The company operates a platform, network and market infrastructure for stablecoin and blockchain applications. One factor weighing on the firm this past quarter was management's announcement that it would offer 10 million Class A shares to the public.

An underweight in Oracle (+29%) was another negative. The primary driver was the business's second-quarter earnings call on September 9, when the company disclosed a massive increase in its cloud unit's remaining performance obligations, sending the stock up nearly 40% the following day. Although some of that gain evaporated before quarter end, most of it remained intact.

Turning to fixed income, the fund's investment-grade bond holdings recorded a gain of 2.02% for Q3, about in line with the 2.03% advance of the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Over the three-month period, investor sentiment shifted from caution to relative optimism, as concerns about U.S. trade and fiscal policy cooled and fixed-income investors welcomed the resumption of policy interest-rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve. On August 1, a new jobs report showed a sharp slowdown in hiring, with the U.S. economy adding just 73,000 jobs in July. Additionally, employment gains for May and June were revised downward. Mid-month, the inflation rate held steady at 2.7%. Meanwhile, corporate earnings remained strong, and the Trump administration's newly implemented tariffs on U.S. trade partners proved less severe and disruptive than the market had initially feared. Taken together, these developments were seen as giving the Fed clearance to resume cutting the federal funds rate at its September meeting, which did in fact occur.

Over the quarter, longer-dated bonds with maturities of 10+ years posted the strongest advance at 3.26%, while intermediate and short-term issues lagged, with 7-10 year bonds gaining 2.58%, 5-7 year maturities up 1.82%, and 1-3 year issues rising 1.20%. From a credit rating standpoint, bonds rated BAA (the Bloomberg equivalent to BBB) outperformed higher-quality issues.

Versus the Bloomberg index, an underweight to mortgage-backed securities detracted in Q3, whereas an overweight to emerging-market debt contributed. Security selection also was positive, while the impact of yield-curve positioning was essentially neutral. ■

Outlook and Positioning

The effect of tariffs, a ballooning federal budget deficit and weakening labor market are all factors that could increase market volatility. As a result, the fund's equity co-managers will likely continue to favor high-quality businesses with idiosyncratic drivers that might be able to sustain growth, even in suboptimal conditions.

We made minor adjustments to the fund's equity sector weightings the past three months but continued to pursue our essentially sector-neutral approach to investing, which allows for meaningful overweights and underweights at the industry and individual stock levels. We remain confident in our investment process, in which a team of sector-focused co-managers are charged with adding value through security selection. Meanwhile, a group leader is responsible for general team oversight, asset allocation, cash management and risk monitoring.

We also follow a very disciplined and methodical process when it comes to the fund's fixed-income holdings. We start with broad risk analysis and a search for value in the bond market, taking a top-down perspective. Once we decide how much risk to take in the portfolio, the next step is to decide which sectors we want to invest in, based on our analysis of the risk/reward tradeoff.

Helping us with this effort are various fixed-income specialists at Fidelity, who regularly provide risk assessments for virtually every investable fixed-income sector we would consider, both in the U.S. and abroad. The last part of the process is picking individual bonds. We methodically scrutinize the fund's investment universe, looking for securities we think are worth purchasing, based on our view of fundamentals, as well as both supply and demand dynamics.

From our perspective, a key consideration as of September 30 is the Federal Reserve's independence. Any meaningful discussion of fixed income must consider the Fed's influence on monetary policy and interest rates. Recently, questions have emerged about its ability to maintain autonomy amid shifting political and economic pressure.

As of quarter end, the portfolio's bond allocations featured an outsized exposure to asset-back securities, with lesser overweights in commercial mortgage-backed securities and Treasurys, the latter of which we added to this quarter. On the other hand, the fund carried a sizable underweight in agency MBS. Meanwhile, we maintained a slightly longer duration compared with the benchmark. ■

Fidelity® Balanced Fund

Investment Approach

Fidelity® Balanced Fund invests across a mix of stocks and bonds – with a 60%/40% neutral allocation – in seeking income and capital growth.

The fund is managed by multiple portfolio managers, with each member dedicated to a particular asset class or market segment, plus a lead manager responsible for determining the appropriate asset allocation, general team oversight, cash management and risk monitoring.

The equity subportfolio is largely sector-neutral and run by a team of sector-focused managers who look to add value through active stock selection.

The investment-grade bond strategy seeks outperformance through sector and security selection and yield-curve positioning. The fund also has a dedicated high-yield bond portfolio, considered out-of-benchmark.

Focused sector and asset class expertise, supported by our deep research infrastructure, is combined with disciplined portfolio construction to provide investment-process consistency in seeking to deliver attractive risk-adjusted returns over time.

Equity Characteristics

Earnings-Per-Share Growth Trailing measures the growth in reported earnings per share over trailing one- and five-year periods.

Earnings-Per-Share Growth (IBES 1-Year Forecast) measures the growth in reported earnings per share as estimated by Wall Street analysts.

Median Market Cap identifies the median market capitalization of the portfolio or benchmark as determined by the underlying security market caps.

Price-to-Book (P/B) Ratio is the ratio of a company's current share price to reported accumulated profits and capital.

Price/Cash Flow is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months cash flow per share.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio (IBES 1-Year Forecast) is the ratio of a company's current share price to Wall Street analysts' estimates of earnings.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio Trailing is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months earnings per share.

Return on Equity (ROE) 5-Year Trailing is the ratio of a company's last five years historical profitability to its shareholders' equity. Preferred stock is included as part of each company's net worth.

Sales-Per-Share Growth measures the growth in reported sales over the specified past time period.

Weighted Average Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the average equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Weighted Median Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the median equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Fixed Income Characteristics

Duration is a measure of a security's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. Duration differs from maturity in that it considers a security's interest payments in addition to the amount of time until

the security reaches maturity, and also takes into account certain maturity shortening features (e.g., demand features, interest rate resets, and call options) when applicable. Securities with longer durations generally tend to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with shorter durations. A fund with a longer average duration generally can be expected to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than a fund with a shorter average duration.

30-day SEC Yield is a standard yield calculation developed by the Securities and Exchange Commission for bond funds. The yield is calculated by dividing the net investment income per share earned during the 30-day period by the maximum offering price per share on the last day of the period. The yield figure reflects the dividends and interest earned during the 30-day period, after the deduction of the fund's expenses. It is sometimes referred to as "SEC 30-Day Yield" or "standardized yield".

30-Day SEC Restated Yield is the fund's 30-day yield without applicable waivers or reimbursements, stated as of month-end.

Net Asset Value is the dollar value of one share of a fund; determined by taking the total assets of a fund, subtracting the total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding.

Indices

Relative Weights

Relative weights represent the % of fund assets in a particular market segment, asset class or credit quality relative to the benchmark. A positive number represents an overweight, and a negative number is an underweight. The fund's benchmark is listed immediately under the fund name in the Performance Summary.

3-Year Risk/Return Statistics

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (leSs) than 1 is more (leSs) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

Yield Curve

The relationship at a given point in time between yields on a group of fixed-income securities with varying maturities - commonly, Treasury bills, notes, and bonds. The curve typically slopes upward since longer maturities normally have higher yields, although it can be flat or even inverted.

data.title: Valterra Platinum: Possible Backpedalled EU ICE Ban
data.description:
Valterra Platinum (ANGPY)(AGPPF) is ultimately more or less a bet on PGM prices, which are constituted by palladium, platinum, and the others that often appear together naturally, have similar functions, properties, and purposes, and trade in a correlated way to each other as well.

Valterra used to be the controlled but independently trading subsidiary of Anglo American (NGLOY), Amplats, or Anglo American Platinum, which has been demerged, and NGLOY has divested their stake in the company. In our last coverage, we mentioned the fact that there may be selling pressure forced on by the demerger. As of after the H1, these latent pressures are gone. So it's not an overhang anymore.

In terms of the drivers of PGM, new trading venues are a first, important element in China. Otherwise, a more pragmatic approach in the EU to the ICE ban plan in the 2030s is being tentatively indicated and could help the PGM outlook, as they are emission reducers for ICEs. As mentioned, no more forced selling overhang. But the stock price has already risen more than palladium and platinum prices, and the company isn't really levered to our understanding. There isn't an especially strong value angle in our view.

Commentary

The only thing to mention from the somewhat stale H1 report is the fact that there were some floods and weather effects that limited mining and reduced it by 15%. This would have factored into the general malaise around the stock as it became sidelined from the Anglo American strategy.

Now the discussion is being dominated by developments in the PGM markets.

  • The GFEX in China has launched a futures and other derivatives exchange for physical commodities like platinum, palladium, and other PGMs for the first time, which is contributing majorly to the liquidity of these products and the underlying commodities. They are more tradable now, being served by an exchange in China, and consequently there has been a positive vector on the commodities.
  • The other thing is to do with the EU. They have quite untenable plans to phase out ICE in the 2030s entirely, given how much of the European industrial footprint, for example, the car majors in Italy and France, have not made major strides in developing EV platforms, and where infrastructure is lagging. This is being reviewed now to allow for the possibility of other offsets to allow the continued existence of ICEs circulating in the EU, which is a good thing for materials like PGMs, which are used to reduce emissions. It's at the stage of "possible review" but markets are liking it. Our understanding is that hybrid models, which may be coming back into fashion as far as solutions to emissions go, require more Platinum than gasoline-based models. This also seems to explain some of the differential between Palladium and Platinum performances - it could be due to this EU factor.

Bottom Line

Our approach here is to look at the relative performances of the spot prices to Valterra's price. Valterra has limited leverage under 0.4x; therefore, there shouldn't be that much additional performance over the commodities. However, this limits the possibility, in our view, of a value case. But it could still be a good vehicle, indeed with an underlying productive operation, to play the PGM price increases should they continue. We think the liquidity considerations should be an enduring backstop to current prices. We also think there could be continued rethinking over the vitalness of electrification of cars (where ICE is an important vertebra in the backbone of Europe's industry) in the EU over other pan-EU objectives, which are perhaps more important now compared to climate, which was more of an issue before the geopolitical environment became more tense.

The risks are, of course, that these are still commodities that depend on vehicle registration rates—newbuilds coming out of factories - which also depend on economic factors. The global economy is a little shaky on the back of questions around the sustainability of US consumption and the effects of US unemployment. So we aren't crazy about betting on cyclical commodities after the run-up.

As described by JPMorgan Asset Management, the fund has an objective of outperforming the ICE BofA US High Yield Constrained Index by investing in…

…all types of high yield, high risk debt securities. The Fund also may invest in convertible securities, preferred stocks, common stocks and privately placed securities.

data.title: Honeywell: Stable Margins Hiding Behind Portfolio Noise
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The investment thesis is built upon the durability of Honeywell's (HON) earnings base, which is comprised of Aerospace aftermarket and service-led automation businesses that continue to provide stable margins and strong cash flow during uneven economic cycles. Near-term revenue and margin variability caused by portfolio changes does not diminish Honeywell's underlying quality and will ultimately enhance it over time. However, Honeywell's valuation continues to represent a generic industrial profile and does not take into consideration Honeywell's durable earnings and exit-rate margins. Both relative and intrinsic analysis support the existence of substantial upside without assuming aggressive growth expectations. Once Honeywell's portfolio has been simplified and normalized profitability is realized, the difference between Honeywell's quality of business and its valuation should begin to close, leading to re-rating based on fundamentals versus market sentiment.

Q3'25 Review and Recent Development

Q3 FY25 revenue at $10.4bn exceeded the analyst consensus of ~$10.15bn and grew ~7% (y/y) (with organic growth of ~6%). Adj. EPS were $2.82 in Q3'25, up about 9% y/y and ahead of analyst expectations. Segment profit was $2.41bn in Q3'25, up about 5% y/y, while consolidated margins of about 23.1% were relatively flat compared to prior years, despite continued cost inflation. Aerospace Technologies performed strongly due to a very active commercial aftermarket, while Industrial Automation experienced a decline in margins due to an adverse product mix. Performance Materials/ESS and Building Automation performed reasonably well, with solid margins achieved.

Honeywell gave full-year 2025 guidance: revenue of between $40.7bn and $40.9bn, representing about 6% organic growth in revenue, and adjusted EPS of between $10.6 and $10.7. Additionally, Honeywell gave guidance for segment profit margins of between 22.9% and 23.0%, suggesting modest downward pressure to near-term margins, as well as guidance of $5.2bn to $5.6bn in free cash flow. On Oct. 30, 2025, Honeywell spun off Solstice Advanced Materials, which is expected to generate about $3.8bn in net sales in 2024. Honeywell also announced in February 2025 that it will spin off its Automation and Aerospace Technologies businesses into two stand-alone companies, both of which should be completed in the second half of 2026.

Revenue Outlook

I believe Honeywell's future growth will be more influenced by the quality of their mix and the leverage they have within their existing installed base rather than the timing of the next economic cycle. As we look at Honeywell's portfolio today, an increasing proportion of its revenues is either recurring, service-based, or related to long-term, multi-year programs. These characteristics make Honeywell's growth relatively more stable regardless of how uneven industrial demand may be during various parts of the cycle.

Aerospace is the largest contributor to the stability of Honeywell's revenue growth. The key driver of aerospace growth is not the number of aircraft being produced, but the size and utilization of the installed base. Flight hours globally are still high, fleets are continuing to age, and delivery delays are causing longer maintenance intervals, all of which are sustaining aftermarket intensity. OEM activity will eventually improve, but it will be secondarily important to service demand when looking at revenue contribution. Additionally, defense and space provide a further layer of stability to aerospace through long-term, budget-backed programs. Collectively, these characteristics cause aerospace growth to be structurally less volatile than most other industrial end markets and allow aerospace to compound steadily throughout the forecast period. Thus, I expect aerospace revenue to grow at a high single-digit rate and to remain at the top of the growth curve among all of Honeywell's segments.

Building Automation is driven by a completely different set of drivers. A larger portion of Building Automation's revenue is now tied to retrofitting, regulatory-mandated upgrades, and service penetration, as opposed to just new construction. Controls, fire, and building solutions are benefiting from both required safety and efficiency regulations, while service and lifecycle activity are smoothing out the natural variability in demand. Although new construction will remain somewhat unpredictable, it will no longer be the dominant factor in determining revenue performance. Therefore, Building Automation is behaving more like a service business based upon an installed base as opposed to a construction-linked cycle. Based on these characteristics, I expect Building Automation to grow at a mid-single-digit rate during the forecast period.

Industrial Automation is likely to be the most sensitive to the immediate industrial environment; however, I believe the revenue base for Industrial Automation is more stable than implied by the recent readings of many industrial purchasing managers' indexes (PMIs). Discrete automation and productivity solutions are being negatively impacted by weaker demand, while the demand for sensing, safety, and workflow solutions, which are being embedded deeper into customers' operations, has been more stable. Most importantly, I do not see Industrial Automation experiencing a "rebound" in demand; I see a stabilization in demand. Therefore, I do not expect Industrial Automation to create a drag on overall Honeywell growth. I expect Industrial Automation to provide incremental revenue growth at a low single-digit rate.

Demand within the Energy & Sustainability Solutions (ESS) segment is currently mixed, resulting in modest growth in FY25. However, once the Solstice Advanced Materials spinoff occurs in FY26, the reported revenue of the remaining ESS segment will experience a structural stepdown, which will be unrelated to cyclical factors. The remaining ESS segment will then be more focused on automation, process solutions, and energy transition projects and will have a cleaner and more predictable revenue base. Although the reported revenue of the remaining ESS segment will experience some impact from the portfolio reset, I expect the underlying demand in the remaining ESS segment to be in the range of low- to mid-single digits.

Overall, I expect Honeywell's consolidated revenue to grow at a mid-single-digit rate in FY25 and to be primarily driven by the strength of the aerospace aftermarket and the stability of Building Automation. I expect the reported revenue to decline at a low single-digit rate in FY26 and to be primarily driven by the Solstice separation. On a like-for-like basis, I expect the remaining segments to continue to grow at a low to mid-single-digit rate. By FY27, I expect the portfolio reset to be complete and for the consolidated revenue of Honeywell to return to a mid-single-digit percent growth rate, consistent with Honeywell's historical through-cycle growth rate.

Margin Outlook

Honeywell's margin outlook for the next few years will be influenced by the earnings mix and portfolio composition, and not by the incremental operating leverage from higher volumes. The margin structure of Honeywell's business is increasingly defined by Aerospace aftermarket and service-led automation businesses, which have historically had and will continue to have structurally higher and more stable margins than Honeywell's legacy industrial portfolio.

In Aerospace, the profitability of the business is determined by the intensity of aftermarket activity and not by the production rates of original equipment manufacturers (OEMs). Since aftermarket activity accounts for a majority of segment profit, the margins of Aerospace are supported by increased global flight hours, the aging of the global fleet, and extended maintenance cycles. While Honeywell's Aerospace segment experiences input cost pressures, it continues to maintain its pricing discipline and implement productivity initiatives, allowing margins to move upward from the mid-26% range to 27% by FY27. This expansion does not require a recovery in aircraft production.

Building Automation has the most stable margin profile among Honeywell's segments. Building Automation's economics are increasingly driven by services, retrofits, and regulation-driven upgrades, which have higher and more predictable margins than new construction. As Honeywell's service penetration continues to increase and maintains its pricing discipline, Building Automation is expected to exit FY25 with margins of approximately 27%, and then remain stable through FY26 and FY27.

Industrial Automation will be the main source of near-term margin pressure. Although demand for discrete automation has weakened and Industrial Automation has an unfavorable mix, it still has a floor of margin due to the installed base in sensing, safety, and workflow solutions and is taking steps to recover to margins of approximately 19.5 to 20% by FY27.

Margins in Energy & Sustainability Solutions are currently being impacted by transitional effects associated with Honeywell's recent portfolio actions. Before the Solstice Advanced Materials separation, margins in ESS declined to the low 23% range in FY25. After the separation, the remaining ESS portfolio will be more focused on automation, UOP, and energy transition projects and will have improved margin predictability, returning to a mid-23% range by FY27.

All, I expect Honeywell to exit FY25 with adjusted EBIT margins in the mid-24% range. In FY26, margins will compress slightly into the high 23% range, based on portfolio mechanics rather than pricing pressures or execution slip-ups. As the portfolio is reset and the mix is normalized, margins should recover to the 24% level by FY27. Throughout the entire period, margins will remain well-supported by mix, services, and aftermarket economics, rather than by cyclically influenced factors.

At current prices, Honeywell trades at approximately 13.4x FY26 estimated EV/EBITDA and declines further to 12.6x when considering FY27 estimates. This is significantly lower than the average peer estimate of approximately 15.4x and significantly lower than premium quality peers, such as RTX Corporation, which is trading at approximately 19x, despite having more exposure to OEM production cycles. Additionally, although Honeywell is trading at a discount to other diversified industrial peers such as Siemens, 3M, and Hitachi, Honeywell offers comparable or better margin stability, and a larger percentage of its earnings is comprised of aftermarket-driven earnings. Similar conclusions are reached when evaluating Honeywell's valuation on a forward earnings basis. At current prices, Honeywell trades at approximately 18.6x FY26e P/E and 17x FY27e P/E, which are slightly lower than peer averages and lower than many peers with more cyclically exposed revenues.

When applying Honeywell's historical average EV/EBITDA multiple of approximately 16x over the last five years, which is consistent with quality large-cap industrials that exhibit durable margins and strong free-cash-flow conversion, it implies an equity value of approximately $245-263 per share over FY26-FY27, resulting in approximately low-20% to low-30% price upside, or mid-20% to mid-30% total return including dividend payments. Importantly, this valuation framework does not assume a premium valuation, only a normalization to what businesses with similar earnings quality normally trade at.

A similar conclusion can be reached using a forward P/E framework. When applying a 23x multiple, which is consistent with Honeywell's historical average and also consistent with peer averages, results in a fair value range of approximately $245-$266 per share over FY26-FY27.

Risk

The primary risk to the thesis is a greater-than-expected normalizing in the Aerospace aftermarket demand. A significant decrease in global flight hours or a quicker-than-expected increase in aircraft deliveries could result in decreased maintenance requirements, thus decreasing the favorable mix that drives the company's group margins and cash flow. The secondary risk to the thesis is related to Honeywell's ability to execute the separation of Solstice Advanced Materials. Although the separation is strategic in nature, any dissynergies, stranded costs, or prolonged inefficiencies associated with the transition could negatively impact near-term margins. Additionally, sustained softness in automation and building markets represents another risk. Extended delays in capital expenditures or retrofit spending within the industrial sector would likely limit the growth of the segments and potentially cap the upside potential of the company, even if Aerospace remains strong. Lastly, maintaining capital allocation discipline is essential to the investment thesis. Any diversion to lower-return acquisitions or increasing balance sheet leverage would reduce the quality premium associated with the thesis.

Conclusion

Honeywell's portfolio management actions are improving the average earnings quality of the company by reducing exposure to lower margin volatility. Despite the fact that Aerospace will provide Honeywell's long-term margin and cash flow stability, Honeywell is still being valued as if it were a cyclical industrial company. There exists a significant opportunity to take advantage of this difference between the company's earnings resilience and how the market is currently valuing them. Since the exit rate margins at Honeywell will remain relatively consistent, the gap between earnings resilience and market expectations will narrow over time, independent of a favorable macroeconomic environment.

data.title: Cardinal Energy: The Risk Rises (Rating And Outlook Downgrade)
data.description:
The last article on Cardinal Energy (CRLFF) covered the secondary recovery and the venture into the thermal business. Neither of these businesses are considered low-cost businesses. Additionally, this is an upstream company with a dividend model that it has made work so far. Nonetheless, the current political uncertainty would argue for heading towards larger and more integrated companies to get past any coming industry challenges. Currently undervalued Cenovus Energy (CVE) would be one of several Canadian ideas that I write about. This is not the kind of company to be invested in when there are threats of trade wars and tariffs while Canada is bargaining with the United States over a new trade agreement.

Stock Price

As an income idea, this stock has long traded in a range. Since the last article the stock has appreciated nicely for an income idea to trade near the top of its trading range.

The biggest change going forward is the end of the thermal construction project that has marked a use of cash flow and debt accumulation for some time. Therefore, as the thermal project begins production in the near future, there will be a switch from the negative cash flow required for the capital phase of the project to the project becoming cash flow positive.

That could potentially provide a temporary boost to the stock price. Whether it is enough to justify continuing to hold the stock a bit longer is an open question.

Earnings

As shown below, there has been some concern about the dividend payout ratio. However, that payout ratio is subject to capital project plans. As noted before, those plans are now proceeding to the production phase of the thermal project.

(Cardinal Energy is a Canadian producer that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

The same press release noted that the thermal project is now proceeding towards production. That will not only add to the volumes produced reported, but it will also increase cash flow in the near future.

The debt ratio is still satisfactory as it is less than the top limit of 1.5. However, commodity prices are weak, and the United States and Canada are bargaining over trade agreements. Usually, the United States is the biggest customer for heavy oil and thermal oil production. So, in the short-term there could be some uncertainty about sales and pricing if that bargaining is not replaced with something that is both constructive and permanent.

Increasing Risk

The increasing risk comes from both of the ideas that secondary recovery is not the lowest cost way to produce (that belongs to the unconventional and conventional producers) combined with the idea that this management wants to expand to more thermal projects.

The secondary recovery projects have been long-term successes because Canada has low costs in the traditional upstream business. Therefore, higher costs for secondary recovery in Canada often still offer competitive costs when compared to the United States business.

That has allowed this company to get considerable mileage out of its dividend model. That is especially true when it was noted that the debt ratio was unusually low. That is likewise considered a necessity for lower cost producers.

Now the debt ratio has risen as the thermal project proceeded with the capital investment phase necessary before startup. But now, production should soon begin to climb on this project to provide cash flow instead of using cash flow.

The issue with the risk arises from the fact that thermal projects, especially new ones, are seldom low cost. Oftentimes, it takes a few add-on projects to lower the cost. On the other hand, the large capital needed upfront to begin one of these projects often assures a decent cash flow even when the project loses money.

The uncertainty caused by threats of trade wars and tariffs could well mean that this project will lose money first before making money. A successful expansion would appear to depend upon the two countries having a constructive and permanent replacement to the current bargaining. But that could take some time.

Instead, there is every possibility that the current political uncertainty could bring on a North American recession marked by weak commodity prices. That could more than cancel out the benefits of additional production to result in at least temporarily reduced cash flow.

When that uncertainty is combined with the recent debt ratio, things could become more challenging than this company is used to.

Specifically, the price of oil is currently below the assumed price of WTI $65. If anything, that oil price is threatening to go lower.

Secondly, the assumed discount often widens during times of weak oil prices. That is bad news for a project that is just starting up after piling up a lot of debt to get started in the first place.

Long-term, the startup of the Trans Mountain Expansion pipeline has kept the discount in check. But it has also caused a rash of projects like this one to start because the profitability of thermal has improved with the startup of the pipeline.

But all those new projects (including this one) threaten to bring back the old conditions. Now admittedly there is talk of still another pipeline to expand export capacity in Canada. But that pipeline has got to move past the talking phase to the actual existence and used phase to be of any help to the long-term outlook here. Good luck with that happening any time soon.

Summary

Cardinal Energy had an income model that was working because it was low debt and the secondary recovery was reasonably profitable. But that model has now materially changed with a much higher (although still acceptable) debt ratio and the entrance into the very volatile thermal business.

For that reason, this stock moves to a hold with a negative outlook until the future clears up somewhat. Personally, I would prefer to be elsewhere even though the track record here of the income model is so far impressive.

Investing is about avoiding risk. Therefore, the need to guess as to whether or not this model succeeds through a potential economic downturn is really more about accepting the risk of guessing correctly. I personally choose to avoid that risk even though others may not worry about it.

There is a lot to like about the company. But not in the current environment.

Risks

Canada and the United States could have a constructive and permanent agreement tomorrow that would allow for a re-evaluation of the situation in a positive direction.

The United States also has issues in that it is attempting to stimulate the economy by borrowing more money when there is already a high debt load. Should the United States reach the end of its borrowing ability at reasonable rates, there could be a reckoning that will not be pleasant for either country.

Even with the increasing deficit and low interest rates, United States consumers are far from happy. Meanwhile the Dallas Federal Reserve Reported upstream industry shrinkage in the third quarter survey and a variety of comments depending upon producer size in the fourth quarter survey. The shrinkage continued in the fourth quarter. This will affect oil supply and hence imports needed from Canada if this continues. This could be a plus or a minus depending upon what happens with the North American economy.

For me, the overall situation is currently too risky for an income idea.
