data.title: Nvidia Has A Cash Problem
data.description:
As hyperscalers and LLM companies spend billions of dollars on new chips to place into their data centers, NVIDIA (NVDA) is cashing in big time from the AI craze.

While NVIDIA has been extremely successful in bringing its products to market just at a time when AI chatbots started to explode in popularity, NVIDIA now has to deal with another problem: it has way too much cash, and it doesn’t know what to do with it.

Cash needs to be invested in the business at high returns on equity in order to prove to the market that it still has ample internal investment opportunities. If cash just stacks on a business’s balance sheet, as it does in the case of NVIDIA, it raises questions about its capital allocation practices and whether or not the company’s high-growth period is already over.

Companies that tend to have a lot of cash on their balance sheets also risk getting pushed into pursuing share repurchases at unreasonable prices. Share buybacks are potentially accretive if they are executed below book value, which obviously is not the case for NVIDIA. Unless NVIDIA finds a way to put its cash to work at higher returns, I see headwinds for the shares in 2026.

Selling GPUs Is Profitable, But What To Do With The Cash?

The data center is where NVIDIA is making its money, and quite a bit of it. NVIDIA is selling Blackwell Graphics Processing Units to data centers, and the business has done so with quite some success. In Q326 NVIDIA generated $51.2 billion in net revenue from selling GPUs in the data center segment. NVIDIA’s GPU-selling segment increased its revenue by $10.1 billion QoQ in Q326, which is due to the successful ramp-up and new shipments of Blackwell GPUs.

According to NVIDIA, the company sees itself still very much at the beginning of the current AI revolution, with agentic AI and physical AI (robots) being the ultimate catalysts for artificial intelligence adoption across the economy. NVIDIA expects exponential growth to be driven by agentic and physical AI.

In the foreseeable future, however, NVIDIA will make the lion's share of its money from selling GPUs. In Q326, 90% of all net revenue came from selling products to data centers.

While NVIDIA still has years of growth left in the tank, the AI chip business is starting to see new challenges, such as what to do with all the cash that it is generating from selling its GPUs.

Nvidia had about $60.1 billion in cash (up $17.4 billion since the end of the 2025 year) just sitting on its balance sheet, which shows that the AI chip business may not have many attractive capital investment opportunities internally. Note, NVIDIA’s cash balance has steadily increased since 2022 as the company found major success with its AI chips for data centers.

NVIDIA uses its cash mainly to repurchase shares, but it is not doing so at great prices. NVIDIA’s NTM P/E ratio is 42.0, which means you are paying 42 times expected earnings for one dollar of NVIDIA earnings.

In the third quarter, NVIDIA repurchased $12.7 billion worth of its own shares, and the repurchase didn’t even make much of a dent in NVIDIA’s cash accounts. Since Q226 NVIDIA’s cash balance has risen $3.8 billion, despite executing almost $13 billion in buybacks. In the entire three quarters of the current fiscal year 2025, Nvidia spent $36.3 billion on share repurchases, or about $12.1 billion on average each month.

I am not convinced that paying 42 NTM earnings is a great bargain for investors, and unless NVIDIA finds higher ROE investment opportunities, the business’s stash of cash could be turning out to be a drag on its earnings growth in 2026.

NVIDIA Needs To Find New Investment Opportunities With High ROEs

I don’t believe that the biggest challenge for the chip business will be to sustain its shipment momentum for products like the Blackwell GPU or server-rack solutions such as the Grace Blackwell NVL 72. NVIDIA is shipping millions of Blackwell GPUs a year now and will do the same with the Ruben GPU that is slated for release next year.

The biggest challenge for NVIDIA will be to invest money with high returns on equity consistently, which is ultimately what matters most to the market. NVIDIA is currently seeing an uplift in returns on equity, which reflects the massive demand for its GPUs. However, if the business fails to funnel new cash into new investment opportunities, it may risk of seeing falling ROEs in the future.

NVIDIA could drive ROE upside in 2026 by:

  • Investing available cash into new AI partnerships that cement durable strategic benefits in the GPUaaS market;
  • Acquiring new AI technology and startups via its venture arm;
  • Becoming more selective when the company executes share buybacks;
  • Utilizing cash to pay a higher dividend, which is something shareholders would surely appreciate;
  • Pursuing acquisition opportunities in the realm of physical AI software and sovereign AI cloud services.

NVIDIA did make a number of high-profile investments in 2025, however, including $100 billion that was invested into OpenAI as well as $5.0 billion invested into Intel (INTC), so the AI business has the option to utilize its cash balance in strategic ways.

According to Pitchbook data, between January and mid-November, NVIDIA invested $23.7 billion in a total of 59 transactions. NVIDIA’s deal-making picked up noticeable steam in 2023 when its cash coffers started to swell from surging sales of data center chips.

While some of the investments have been criticized due to the inherent circularity of the deals (e.g. NVIDIA invests in OpenAI which uses the money to buy chips from the company), there are just not that many attractive, big deals out there, and AI companies are, generally speaking, expensive.

NVIDIA does have a big stake in CoreWeave, however, in which the chip company owns 24.2 million shares, valued at $2.0 billion. It is likely that management will continue to work closely with GPUaaS companies (and invest in them) in order to build out their AI infrastructure offers, but the question of how to deploy $60 billion effectively still lingers.

My Game Plan And Fair Value Estimate

NVIDIA has a Price-To-Sales Ratio of 13.3 vs. 5.7 for Nebius (NBIS) and 2.8 for CoreWeave. NVIDIA is also the only business here that can be valued with a traditional P/E as both Nebius and CoreWeave are not profitable. NVIDIA, however, is a preferred partner for both GPUaaS businesses, and the company gives both businesses early access to new Graphics Processing Units, which means all companies discussed here benefit from growing demand for chips, they just benefit differently.

NVIDIA sells GPUs to both companies and data centers, which in turn rent access to computing power to their own clients. NVIDIA is the ultimate beneficiary of growing demand for LLM-capable chips, while companies like Nebius and CoreWeave benefit by leasing them under their own respective GPUaaS models.

NVIDIA is obviously trading at a big premium here compared to peers, which reflects the business’s strong execution in the GPU market at a time of heightened demand. In my opinion, NVIDIA could be priced at 23-25 times FWD earnings (=Fair Value Estimate of $172-187 a share), which I believe would reflect the business’s strong GPU product portfolio as well as surging demand for Blackwell GPUs.

Since shares now already trade at 23 FWD earnings, I believe the upside is currently constrained, and I would not risk a position in NVIDIA at today’s prices. However, I would definitely consider buying NVIDIA on a pullback, say below $150 a share. This would allow for a higher margin of safety and possibly improve my risk equation quite a bit.

Suitability, Risks To Consider

Nvidia is a multi-product AI company with considerable investments in software and hardware products, which broadens the appeal of the chip company. It offers the entire software AI stack as well as critical hardware products, like its GPU suite, to serve the enterprise and consumer end markets.

However, Nvidia is not utilizing its cash effectively, in my opinion. Hoarding a lot of cash is set to be a drag on NVIDIA’s earnings growth, which may become a major challenge for the business in 2026. Capital always needs to be invested in order to earn an adequate, risk-adjusted return for investors. If cash sits idly on a business’s balance sheet and is not deployed for the benefit of shareholders, NVIDIA might not see many attractive investment opportunities itself.

My Verdict: Hold

Nvidia is growing at a dizzying pace, but while it is a good problem to have to be awash in cash, it also creates problems: NVIDIA will have to show investors that it can invest its cash at high internal rates of return to generate more shareholder value in the future or risk slowing earnings growth.

If Nvidia fails to deploy more cash into high-return opportunities, the business may continue to sit on billions of cash that are earning only a meager return. I also doubt whether share buybacks at a 42 NTM P/E ratio are really a good use of shareholder funds.

A better use of capital might be to acquire new startups with unique AI applications in order to complement NVIDIA’s existing data center business or broaden its appeal by striking major partnerships with robotics companies in order to rapidly scale physical AI products.

If NVIDIA finds higher-return investment opportunities to deploy its cash against in 2026, I can see a path for a higher growth premium. As long as NVIDIA sits on $60 billion worth of cash, and growing, I believe a buy rating at this moment in time is not deserved.

data.title: Amazon: A Correction Under $200 May Be Necessary Before Uptrend Resumes (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
I haven't mentioned Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) (AMZN:CA) in a while on Seeking Alpha. Personally, I have avoided the stock for years because of its overvaluation problem (writing mostly bearish takes, explaining the risk side of trading AMZN). To a degree, I am at it again in this article, explaining the odds of a short-term correction before a rebound can materialize later in 2026.

In terms of vindication for investing elsewhere, Amazon has severely underperformed both the NASDAQ 100 and S&P 500 indexes for a total return over the past 12-month and 5-year periods. Below are comparisons matching Amazon against owning the peer Big Tech Invesco QQQ (QQQ) and SPDR S&P 500 (SPY) ETFs.

However, I will say the valuation picture has improved slightly going into 2026. Today, both Walmart (WMT) here and Costco (COST) here appear to be more overvalued than Amazon in the major U.S. retailing sector. So, with better projected growth rates than peers, I am going to actually upgrade my long-term rating to Hold.

For a fundamental analysis summary of my upgrade decision, 2026's estimated P/E of 29x on an expected growth curve for EPS of 15% annually produces a forward PEG ratio under 2.0x. Not exactly a bargain basement setting, but perhaps the most acceptable valuation backstop for the stock since the end of 2022.

However, I do find serious problems in the technical trading pattern, which have been compounding since July. A rare (for Amazon) momentum downturn may be signaling a price drop below $200 into early 2026 as the next minor move.

Consequently, I would wait for a -10% to -20% correction to play out before committing new capital to AMZN.

Let me explain what I am looking at, creating the need for patience from shareholders and new buyers of the stock.

Technical Weakness Warning

Since October, a variety of unusual technical circumstances have come into view. Basically, lagging relative price action vs. the NASDAQ 100 over the trailing period of one year and a downturn in the Accumulation/Distribution Line, in conjunction with incredible weakness in the 14-day Ease of Movement indicator (reaching or close to all-time lows at the time), have only appeared twice over the past 20 years.

The two previous instances of these indicators turning lower at the same moment were in early 2014 and 2022. Both of the bearish technical trading backgrounds preceded greater levels of selling and price decline in Amazon. 2014 proved the mild selloff example, while 2022 was more of the investor wipeout case.

2025 Trading History

Below is a 12-month chart of where we stand today. You will notice relative strength vs. QQQ peaked last February, the Acc/Dist Line rolled over in July, and the EMV sell signal appeared a few weeks ago in November (circled in gold). The EMV reading essentially tied the all-time low number from March. I would note: AMZN has steadily underperformed the NASDAQ 100 since the March EMV record low.

2014 & 2022 Parallels

If you go back through the last two decades, late 2025's downward direction in the above-mentioned indicators just doesn't happen very often. Either Amazon has been outperforming the market, or the stock has been under real accumulation, or shorter-term money inflows have been evident in the EMV calculation.

Yet, in early 2014, the stock experienced a significant price dump, triggering new all-time lows in the EMV during February and April (circled in gold below). The Acc/Dist Line was generally trending higher during this period but did exhibit a downturn in March-April. From that point, the stock would zigzag lower the rest of 2014 into January 2015, with bottoms in price during May, October, and January around -15% lower.

Late 2020 into November 2021 experienced a flatlining AMZN share quote with increasingly weaker relative strength vs. peer Big Tech names, on top of rotten Acc/Dist Line action into January 2022. At that point, an imbalance of selling vs. buying volumes appeared as an all-time low EMV reading. New all-time lows in the EMV were then successively reached in March and again in May (all circled in gold below). Following the February rebound in price, Amazon would decline -50% in price into January 2023's eventual bottom.

I will say today's setup for these indicators is not as wickedly bad as early 2022 but does look materially worse than the 2014 instance. Perhaps we get a price downmove in between these two examples, in the -15% to -20% range for AMZN over the next 3-4 months, as a guesstimate.

Final Thoughts

For a review, if Amazon's price does fall under $200 in a -15% corrective move in the first quarter of 2026, a much stronger buy proposition may open for savvy investors. For a target price as technical support, $194 would fill a daily trading gap from May 2025.

Assuming operating business growth forecasts remain unchanged long term (for example, we avoid a recession in consumer/business spending next year), a sub-$200 quote could bring a forward PEG ratio closer to 1.6x to 1.8x. PEG scores closer to 1.0x are historically considered Strong Buy territory by Wall Street in any equity. In addition, if the "risk-free" Treasury yield curve drops next year to rates well under 4%, Amazon's earnings yield potentially above 4% by 2027 (P/E under 25x) may offer better support for shareholder worth.

The technical trading evidence is suggesting a weaker underpinning for the stock now exists vs. what Amazon has typically witnessed in the past. Plus, the U.S. equity market in general continues to trade at an unsustainable valuation level (in my view, trading the markets for almost 40 years).

Heavy insider selling at Amazon is continuing in late 2025, highlighting that management is anxious to liquidate at least a portion of their holdings to lock in gains. If there's any silver lining, it's the fact that sell blocks over the past three months have primarily been in the millions of dollars from managers and hundreds of millions from founder Jeff Bezos (not billions of dollars).

One bullish scenario I am running through my research is that Amazon may be able to "outperform" the NASDAQ 100 and S&P 500 indexes for a number of years after a minor -10% to -20% correction phase appears soon. I might even put a Buy rating on the stock if a serious drawdown becomes reality in 2026.

For aggressive traders, a short-term sell or short position is one option. However, I do feel the Amazon valuation could support a move above $300 in coming years. Another possibility: AMZN's price may flatline in the low-$200s for some time, as the overall Wall Street health condition becomes more precarious with the vast majority of equities falling in price.

When I bring all of the pros (improving fundamental valuation) and cons (bearish momentum) together, any outlook or forecast rating beyond six months may be best put as "Neutral" or "Hold." I would wait for price weakness before buying a new position or adding to your Amazon stake. However, rushing to sell may not prove the wisest course of action, especially if you are holding large taxable gains. A prudent middle-ground strategy may include selling covered calls as a price hedge and income generator.

At least, that's the way I see Amazon's position for investors currently.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: ISCF: Japan-Heavy ETF With Banner 2025, Yet Skeptical Stance Is Warranted For 2026
data.description:
Today, I would like to initiate coverage of the iShares International Small-Cap Equity Factor ETF (ISCF), a passively managed exchange-traded fund offering exposure to developed-world small-cap equities selected using a sophisticated factor-centered process, with a Hold rating.

The fact is, the Japan- and UK-heavy ISCF is wrapping up 2025 on an extremely optimistic note, beating the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) by about 14.9%, in part supported by the impressive performance of the euro, the pound sterling, the Australian dollar, etc.

This is a beautiful example of how being a contrarian (and venturing into the high-risk universe of developed-world small caps amid the U.S. market rally is a perfect example of non-mainstream thinking) may actually reward investors.

However, my goal today is not to talk about how generous ISCF's returns have been in 2025. The question is what the year 2026 will bring for international small caps, and I would dare to assume that the case for ISCF (and for its peers as well) beating the S&P 500 ETF for the second year in a row is implausible, as the data to construct an overly optimistic hypothesis like that is lacking, plus I do not anticipate the forces that contributed to its return in 2025 to continue pushing it higher with a similar energy in 2026.

So below, I would like to give due attention to ISCF's strategy, performance, and portfolio composition, as well as offer some context on its peers, to explain why I am modestly skeptical about this vehicle.

ISCF Key Facts

  • Inception: April 28, 2015
  • Investment Adviser: BlackRock Fund Advisors
  • AUM: $539.87 million, as of December 24
  • Turnover: 30%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.24%

ISCF Strategy Review

As we know from the prospectus available on the iShares website, the STOXX International Small-Cap Equity Factor Index is the core of its strategy. However, an important nuance can be found in the fact sheet. The gist is that the ETF tracked a different index before March 1, 2023, namely the MSCI World ex USA Small Cap Diversified Multiple-Factor Index (Net). Even though some methodological overlap can be identified between the indices (i.e., their focus on developed-world small caps and the inclusion of factor considerations related to value, momentum, and quality), they are not exactly the same, and I believe for the sake of accuracy, we need to focus on the ETF's returns since the strategy change (a quick look at the returns prior to that makes sense nonetheless).

As described in the prospectus, ISCF's underlying index comprises small-cap names from the STOXX International Developed Markets Small Cap Index that are identified and assigned weights "using an optimization process designed to maximize exposure to four target factors: momentum, quality, value, and low volatility." As we know from the STOXX website,

The STOXX International Developed Markets Small Cap Index is a broad market cap weighted index designed to represent the performance of the Small Cap companies from Developed Markets excluding US covering approximately 15% of investable market capitalization.

The U.S. equities are ignored, while the Canadian ones are welcome. An important detail I find necessary to highlight is that, as per the STOXX Country Classification, South Korea is considered an emerging market. The same is fair for the MSCI (MSCI) Market Classification. So the ETF should have had no exposure to the Korean equities when it tracked the MSCI index. However, the S&P Developed ex US Broad Market Index (Net), with which the ETF compares its returns as per page S-30 of the prospectus, does include Korean companies, and in the SPDR® Portfolio Developed World ex-US ETF (SPDW), they had a 5.24% weight as of December 22.

As to the frequency of adjustments, the STOXX International Small-Cap Equity Factor Index has a quarterly review and rebalancing schedule. The weighting process is guided by a few constraints. More specifically:

The maximum weight of a single security is 10%, and the sum of security weights that are individually greater than 4.5% must be less than 22.5% of the Underlying Index. The Index Provider also applies other constraints, such as country and sector exposures relative to the Parent Index, among others.

ISCF Portfolio Analysis

As of December 22, there were 1,097 equities in the ISCF portfolio, with the main ten accounting for 5.67% of the net assets. As we can see in the holdings dataset that is available on the iShares website, other assets in the portfolio were cash, cash collateral and margins, futures, FX, and a money market fund.

Ticker (as per the ISCF dataset) U.S. Ticker Company Sector Weight, %, as of December 22 Location
ABN (AAVMY) ABN AMRO Bank N.V. Financials 0.94 The Netherlands
SAB (BNDSY) Banco de Sabadell, S.A. Financials 0.9 Spain
BMPS (BMDPF) Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena S.p.A. Financials 0.71 Italy
LOGN (LOGI) Logitech International S.A. Information Technology 0.52 Switzerland
FTT (FINGF) Finning International Inc. Industrials 0.46 Canada
SQN (SWQGF) Swissquote Group Holding SA Financials 0.46 Switzerland
CG (CGAU) Centerra Gold Inc. Materials 0.42 Canada
DBI None Dalrymple Bay Infrastructure Limited Industrials 0.42 Australia
HBAN (HLVTY) Helvetia Baloise Holding AG Financials 0.42 Switzerland
ACLN (ACLLY) Accelleron Industries AG Industrials 0.42 Switzerland

Created by the author using data from the ETF and Seeking Alpha

To offer a glimpse into how ISCF's top holdings have been doing in 2025, I should mention that ABN AMRO Bank N.V., an Amsterdam-based bank with a market cap of about €18.1 billion, had a blockbuster year, delivering a close to 96.6% price return in Amsterdam as of writing this article. Banco de Sabadell, S.A., with a market cap of about €16.8 billion, was similarly successful, delivering an approximately 80% return in Madrid.

Regarding sectors, ISCF does have a few similarities to its peers, including the Schwab International Small-Cap Equity ETF (SCHC), the iShares MSCI EAFE Small-Cap ETF (SCZ), the SPDR® S&P® International Small Cap ETF (GWX), and the Schwab Fundamental International Small Equity ETF (FNDC), as well as SPDW, but their preferences are not entirely identical.

For example, compared to SPDW, it is significantly overweight in real estate and materials, among others, while underweight in financials and health care, to name a few. Compared to SCHC, it has more confidence in consumer discretionary and financials while underweighting a few sectors, including materials and health care.

Speaking of countries, most of its net assets are allocated to Japanese, UK, Canadian, and Australian stocks that combined account for 54.6% of the net assets. My calculations show that the top 20 countries out of 27 it has exposure to (as per the dataset) account for 98.67% of the portfolio.

The main difference from SPDW's country allocation is that ISCF ignores South Korea. Other notable differences are that ISCF is overweight in Japanese, UK, and Australian equities, while underweight in Canadian, Swiss, German, and French, among others.

ISCF Performance

As I said above, in 2025, ISCF has clearly had a moment.

It is still of note, though, that it has underperformed such peers as FNDC, GWX, and SCHC, as well as SPDW, beating only SCZ. Anyway, an over 34% total return is still impressive.

What factors helped it to beat the U.S. market I proxied with IVV? I believe the primary one was the tariff drama-related volatility that reverberated through currencies and equities, and especially the depreciation of the U.S. dollar vs. the euro, the Swiss franc, and the pound sterling. Also, propelled by the hawkishness of the Bank of Japan, the yen was a massive factor for ISCF and its Japan-heavy peers earlier this year, but its impact dropped to near zero in the months that followed.

The fact is, about 84.9% of ISCF's net assets are allocated to stocks priced in six currencies, namely the EUR, JPY, GBP, CAD, AUD, and CHF. As we can see from the performance of the Invesco CurrencyShares® Euro Currency Trust ETF (FXE), the Invesco CurrencyShares® British Pound Sterling Trust ETF (FXB), the Invesco CurrencyShares® Japanese Yen Trust ETF (FXY), the Invesco CurrencyShares® Australian Dollar Trust ETF (FXA), the Invesco CurrencyShares® Swiss Franc Trust ETF (FXF), and the Invesco CurrencyShares® Canadian Dollar Trust ETF (FXC), all these currencies have gained vs. the USD. However, I do not expect these factors to remain that supportive in 2026.

Next, zooming in on the monthly returns, we see that most of ISCF's outperformance happened in the first half of the year (February–May).

Month ISCF Active Return (IVV used as a benchmark)
January -0.7%
February 2.2%
March 7.0%
April 6.1%
May 0.2%
June -0.3%
July -2.6%
August 2.3%
September -1.5%
October -3.5%
November 1.2%

Calculated by the author using data from Portfolio Visualizer

And while it is ahead of IVV YTD, it has trailed it by about 2.5% in the last six months.

Second, the data for March 2023–November 2025 does not look convincing at all. On a side note, I did not add FNDC to the comparison, as it changed its index in June 2024.

We see a few issues here.

  • ISCF underperformed not only the U.S. market proxied with IVV but also SPDW and SCHC.
  • It captured about 101.65% of SPDW's downside but only 96.69% of its upside.
  • Its risk-adjusted returns measured using the Sharpe and Sortino ratios were softer than IVV's and SPDW's, though it did outmaneuver international small-cap peers.

Besides, while we have just 33 full calendar months of ISCF's performance with the new index, we can take a look at its performance prior to the change, since, as I said above, there is some methodological overlap between the indices.

So my calculations show that over May 2015–February 2023, ISCF delivered a 5.37% annualized total return, close to 2x lower than IVV. Still, it managed to outmaneuver SPDW, which delivered just 3.74% CAGR.

Moreover, nothing can hinder us from taking a look at other international small-cap plays like SCHC, which has a much longer history going back to the post-GFC global markets recovery. The fact is its returns before 2025 do not look convincing at all. For instance, it had seven years of underperformance in a row (2018–2024), and since its inception in 2010, it has never had two consecutive years of outperformance. It beat IVV only in February–December 2010, 2012, 2015, 2017, and January–November 2025.

Year SCHC Active Return (IVV used as a benchmark)
February–December 2010 8.80%
2011 -18.73%
2012 3.13%
2013 -10.90%
2014 -20.83%
2015 1.00%
2016 -9.17%
2017 7.63%
2018 -14.16%
2019 -8.17%
2020 -7.93%
2021 -16.73%
2022 -3.58%
2023 -11.97%
2024 -22.95%
January–November 2025 17.12%

Prepared by the author using data from Portfolio Visualizer

As of writing this article on December 24, I see that SCHC is beating IVV by close to 19% in 2025. In fact, this is its best year since its inception.

So my point here is that considering no new phase of the USD depreciation (and I do not expect one), there is little to no possibility of ISCF delivering a higher total return than IVV's in 2026.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, with its multifactor take on developed-world small caps, ISCF has been on a tear in 2025, beating not only SCZ but also the S&P 500 ETF. I admit that this is a decent momentum story, but thinking that 2026 should be another alpha year is counterintuitive, and there is no historical data to back up this assumption. I also do not expect the FX factor to be as supportive as in 2025. So more likely its performance will revert back to what we saw in 2023 and 2024. What should not change, though, is ISCF's high downside capture ratio.

data.title: TJX Companies: Rating Downgrade On Expensive Valuation
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating for The TJX Companies (TJX) two years ago in April '24, as I saw potential for TJX to beat its FY25 guidance and that it would drive multiples higher. Fast forward to today, the fundamental story has gotten better. HomeGoods has pivoted toward frequency, the customer base is skewing younger, and international expansion is going well. Fundamentally, the business is stronger than it was two years ago. The question is whether the current valuation already reflects most of that, and I think it already has. I downgrade to hold.

The Pivot to Frequency is Working (HomeGoods)

One very interesting point I observed with TJX since then was that the HomeGoods banner had shifted away from a discretionary and cyclical shopping destination toward something that will capture consumer traffic all year round. For perspective, in the latest Q3 2026 quarter, TJX managed to deliver 5% CSG. And I find this very bullish because this is a quarter that is typically quiet for the home category. Remember that in Q3, there is no spring renovation benefit and also no holiday gifting tailwind in Q3, so when HomeGoods posts that level of CSG, we know for a fact that this banner is seeing demand from elsewhere.

My belief is that the mix shift toward consumables, snacks, and giftable items is delivering as expected. I really find this to be a smart strategy because these categories pull customers in more frequently and for smaller, repeat purchases. Instead of waiting for the occasion where a consumer needs to buy a new couch or renovate their home, which are very infrequent occasions, having these new, smaller consumable items gives shoppers a reason to stop by more frequently (even daily). The general idea here is that, over time, customer lifetime value for this HomeGoods banner improves because more visits mean a higher likelihood of a purchase. It also helps with stabilizing the revenue profile since customers are coming in more frequently.

The knock-on impact from these is also that valuation should get better. My view is that home retailers have typically traded at a discount because revenue tends to be lumpy and tied to housing cycles. That was the old HomeGoods banner. But with the shift now, HomeGoods has a steadier demand pattern profile, which allows for better inventory planning and labor utilization (it's easier to plan how many labors needed for each store). All of these should lead to the market rewarding the stock with a higher multiple.

Capturing the Youth

In my view, the long-term growth for TJX is really dependent on whether it can successfully pivot the brand to capture the younger crowd. This is especially true for TJX because this is a brand with a long operating history, which means the bulk of its customers have aged together with the brand. If the customer base eventually ages out, the “terminal value” for TJX gets impaired since the brand could become irrelevant.

The good news is that TJX has managed to pivot. Management has been explicit that customer acquisition continues to skew toward Gen Z and Millennials. This is the most important data point that we need to prove that TJX is lowering the “age” of its customer base, and that itself materially lowers the long-term risk profile. The “how” matters here because it shows why this pivot is structural. What management did was to adjust its marketing strategy. Instead of recycling marketing at the same seasonal spots, they have reinvested in channels that over-index with younger consumers. A few good examples are sponsorships tied to women's sports and targeted podcast advertising. And I think the logic makes sense here because, as TJX, you want to introduce the off-price value proposition early (when consumers are young) so that their spending habits will include purchasing off-price items. Once TJX can help form that habit early, it effectively captures a customer for the many years to come.

Global Growth, the New Growth Lever

TJX's international expansion is a growth driver that one shouldn't ignore. In the latest call, management confirmed plans to enter Spain with the TK Maxx banner in early 2026. We've already seen the concept work in the UK, Ireland, Germany, and Poland. But Spain's outlet-centre footprint per capita is below more developed European off-price markets like the UK and Italy, and that gives TJX a longer runway to capture “new demand” without competing with incumbents. Also, Spain would be the first Western European country for TJX. If this concept works, it makes the case for broader Western European expansion more plausible.

There is also a less obvious benefit that matters, and that is vendor relationships. With a wider international footprint, it allows TJX to clear inventory on a global scale. This is a huge advantage against local sub-scale players because it would be a position of strength when it comes to acquiring excess merchandise, particularly from international brands looking to clear high-volume inventories. Over time, with more countries, it means deeper vendor ties, which gives TJX better access to product. This is the same flywheel effect we see in the US, where more stores create more buying power. More buying power improves inventory availability. Better inventory drives traffic and transaction growth. That loop becomes harder to replicate as TJX scales.

Valuation

All is good from a fundamental basis, but the valuation scares me. TJX is trading at 30x forward PE, which is close to the largest premium vs. peers. And this is despite TJX's revenue growth outlook being similar to Ross Stores (ROST), which is at a high-single-digit percentage. But ROSS trades at 25x forward PE. I get the attractiveness of TJX penetrating the younger generation consumer cohort and Spain expansion, but at 30x, the upside doesn't seem attractive to me. Let's just take consensus FY28 adj. EPS estimates to anchor expectations. They estimate an adj. EPS of $5.73. At 30x, that equates to a share price of $171, a 10% upside over 2 years (~5% a year).

Conclusion

My view for TJX is now a hold. It is executing well by addressing HomeGoods issue, capturing younger age demographics, and penetrating global markets. However, at ~30x forward earnings, much of that upside looks priced in. Using consensus FY28 adjusted EPS of $5.73, the implied return is not attractive at all.

data.title: AAR: Firing On All Cylinders
data.description:
In August, I concluded that shares of AAR (AIR) were cruising to fresh highs. The company was delivering strong revenue and earnings growth following the integration of the acquisition of Triumph.

With elevated leverage rapidly coming down, there are some drivers for continued earnings growth in 2026, and even as shares were trading near their all-time highs, more momentum was seen.

Momentum and news flow quickly arrived in the fall, after AAR announced no less than 3 deals while offering some equity to keep leverage in check as well. Such moves and continued operating momentum make me happy to maintain a position, even at current higher levels.

Aviation Aftermarket Services

AAR provides aviation aftermarket services, with the business founded soon after World War II. Such products and services include parts, integrated solutions, expeditionary services, and repair & engineering services.

Positioned as an independent provider of such services, the company employs over 6,000 workers who provide such services and products across the globe. AAR serves commercial carriers as well as defense and governmental agencies, although the commercial market makes up about two-thirds of sales.

By now the company generates about $2.7 billion in sales, with parts supply being the largest segment, making up about 40% of sales. This is largely complemented by repair & engineering and integrated solutions, with expeditionary services having become a minimal unit with the passage of time. The repair & engineering units have seen a considerable bump following a $725 million deal to acquire Triumph, a transaction announced late in 2023.

The company is operating in a massive, nearly $140 billion global MRO market, which provides continued growth and consolidation opportunities, coming on top of solid growth achievements already delivered. Over the past decade, the company has nearly doubled sales, with these sales obviously taking a dip during the pandemic. Operating margins have risen from mid-single digits to high single digits, all amidst a pretty stable share count.

About The Valuation

In July of this year, AAR reported a 20% increase in fiscal 2025 sales to $2.78 billion, as EBITDA was up 34% to $324 million. Net debt was reported at $880 million, for a leverage ratio reported at 2.7 times. All this allowed for adjusted earnings of $3.91 per share.

While no formal 2026 guidance was issued, the company struck an upbeat tone, as the company announced a $15 million deal to acquire Aerostrat in August, adding long-range maintenance planning software, as this was truly a bolt-on transaction.

Trading at $77 in August, shares traded at 19 times trailing earnings, which were seen advancing further in 2026, certainly as the company has been incurring substantial interest expenses on its debt load. This could allow for real deleveraging amidst debt refinancing and debt reduction. I was working with a $4.25-$4.50 per share earnings number in the fiscal year 2026. With leverage seen in the low 2s, and earnings multiples seen at 17-18 times, shares looked fairly valued, with potential for shares to continue to fly higher going forward.

Eventful Times

Since August, it has been busy on the corporate front for AAR. In September, the company reported a solid 12% increase in first-quarter sales to $740 million. Margins kept improving, with EBITDA up 18% to $87 million, as adjusted earnings rose some 27% to $1.08 per share. Net debt actually ticked up to $942 million, yet with EBITDA trending around $337 million, that was not a concern, as the strong start made me feel really comfortable with the earnings numbers mentioned above.

Days later, AAR announced the acquisition of American Distributors Holding, a distributor of components and assemblies, in a $146 million cash deal. Founded in 1983, ADI employs about 400 workers, who generate $149 million in sales and $15 million in EBITDA, revealing that a 1 times sales multiple and nearly 10 times EBITDA multiple have been paid.

With pro forma net debt seen up to $1.09 billion, the company sold 3 million shares towards the end of the month. At an offer price of $83 per share, this yields a quarter of a billion in gross proceeds, for pro forma net debt around $850 million. Note that some dilution will be incurred, with the transaction seen increasing the count from about 36 million shares to 39 million shares, yet it allows for the purchase of ADI and net deleveraging at the same time.

Aided by this influx, AAR announced the purchase of HAECO Americas in a $78 million cash deal, adding to the repair & engineering segment, although no further financial details have been announced. Just before the year end, AAR announced a $35 million cash deal to acquire Aircraft Reconfig Technologies to expand engineering and certification capabilities, with again no financial details being announced.

In between the three deals and the share issuance, I peg the net debt load at just over $950 million, as I see leverage ratios in the mid-2s. The company has incurred about 8% dilution here, but the ADI deal alone will boost sales by about 5%, with an unknown revenue contribution from the most recent two deals.

And Now?

Following an acquisitive period, which seems to have been paid for with higher-valued shares, I think that AAR is doing just fine here. Shares have seen further gains, now trading at $85 per share, trading within imminent sight of the highs around $90 per share.

Following the strong start to the year and the M&A moves over the fall, I feel very comfortable working with a $4.25-$4.50 per share estimate for the year, potentially seeing upside to that. This now pushes up forward earnings multiples to 19-20 times earnings, with upside to positive delivery. For all that, I anxiously look forward to learning more about the second quarter earnings report.

Hence, I continue to see AAR operating from a position of strength and applaud this. Having held a small position, I am backing the recent moves made, while the business itself does just fine, making me a patient holder of the stock.
